name: triple sword
shortDescription: A three-bladed sword built more for spectacle and intimidation than ordinary fencing. Buyers argue over whether it is practical, but no one argues that it looks expensive when set across velvet in a weapons hall.
flavorText: “Some blades are made to cut. This one is made to end conversations before they begin.”

name: tsurugi
shortDescription: A straight, old-pattern blade with a severe profile and little patience for ornament. Its value lies in balance, age, and the quiet confidence of a weapon that was respected before newer fashions learned to shine.
flavorText: “The old sword does not explain itself. It simply waits for the hand that understands.”

name: two handed sword
shortDescription: A long, weighty sword meant for committed swings and strong arms, with a grip broad enough for both hands and no room for hesitation. Mercenaries prize it for reach; collectors prize it for the dents that prove it was not merely hung on a wall.
flavorText: “A blade this large teaches manners from several paces away.”

name: ceremonial cutlass
shortDescription: A parade cutlass with polished fittings, a curved blade, and just enough edge to remind guests that ceremony and threat often share a tailor. It sells well to captains, harbor officials, and nobles who want their maritime stories believed.
flavorText: “For salutes, speeches, and the rare dinner where someone must be impressed quickly.”

name: fabled sword of doom
shortDescription: A grim fabled sword whose dark reputation arrives before the blade does. Merchants handling it speak softly, not because steel can listen, but because some customers pay more when fear is allowed to settle over the table.
flavorText: “Every doom needs a witness. This one prefers a wielder.”

name: fabled blade of flaming death
shortDescription: A fabled blade said to carry the temper of old fire, its edge linked to battlefield songs, tavern lies, and charred marks no cleaner has fully explained. Dangerous buyers love the name; cautious merchants love charging extra for the locked case.
flavorText: “It is amazing how quickly a room grows quiet around a sword with too many adjectives.”

name: fabled sword of Reshaping
shortDescription: A strange fabled sword associated with alteration, warped bargains, and stories of enemies who left duels as very different people. Whether those tales are true matters less than the way scholars and warlocks lean in when the name is spoken.
flavorText: “Change is easier to sell when it has a handle.”

name: scarce katana of speed
shortDescription: A scarce katana balanced with unnerving lightness, favored by duelists who value a clean draw and a faster boast. The blade’s worth depends on its condition, but its reputation depends on how quickly a buyer believes it can move.
flavorText: “Fast steel makes slow apologies unnecessary.”

name: ironclan sword
shortDescription: A hard, practical sword from Ironclan hands, built with more discipline than decoration. Its simple finish hides excellent workmanship, which is exactly how Ironclan smiths prefer their pride to travel through the market.
flavorText: “No flourish, no vanity, no wasted metal. Very Ironclan.”

name: vampires tooth
shortDescription: A sinister little blade or fang-shaped relic traded under a name that makes priests frown and collectors smile. Whether it came from a monster, a tomb, or a very dramatic smith, it carries the perfect amount of unease for rare-curio pricing.
flavorText: “The bite is mostly in the story, which is usually where the profit lives.”

name: wood sword
shortDescription: A plain wooden sword used for training, children’s games, stage work, and occasionally disputes that should have stayed harmless. It is cheap, durable, and surprisingly important in towns where every master-at-arms begins with splinters.
flavorText: “Before steel teaches fear, wood teaches where the hands should go.”

name: wyrmbane
shortDescription: A named blade with a reputation tied to scaled horrors and old hunting companies. Even buyers who have never seen a wyrm inspect it with reverence, because weapons made for impossible quarry always make ordinary dangers feel smaller.
flavorText: “A sword for those who consider dragons an accounting problem.”

name: blowgun
shortDescription: A slim blowgun made for quiet hunting, careful aim, and customers who prefer distance over drama. It is light to carry, easy to hide among trade goods, and best sold with honest warnings about breath, patience, and poor judgment.
flavorText: “It makes very little noise, which is why everyone nearby becomes nervous.”

name: bullwhip
shortDescription: A long braided bullwhip suited to herders, caravan guards, stage performers, and anyone who needs a sharp report without drawing steel. Good leatherwork matters here; a cheap whip embarrasses the buyer before it frightens anyone else.
flavorText: “The crack sells the first one. The wrist sells the second.”

name: fancy bullwhip
shortDescription: A showy bullwhip with polished fittings and carefully worked leather, made for those who want control to look elegant. It draws attention at auctions, especially from performers, nobles with bad hobbies, and caravan masters with excellent posture.
flavorText: “Practical enough to sting, polished enough to boast.”

name: ceremonial blowgun
shortDescription: A decorated blowgun made for ritual display, courtly hunting customs, or collectors of weapons that seem too quiet for their own good. The carvings matter as much as the bore, and buyers will pay dearly if both are clean.
flavorText: “A whispering weapon dressed for a public occasion.”

name: fabled ash fiend whip
shortDescription: A fabled whip associated with ash fiends, scorched contracts, and handlers who wore gloves for reasons they refused to explain. It is more legend than tool in most markets, which naturally makes it easier to price like a disaster.
flavorText: “Some relics smell faintly of smoke no matter how far they are kept from fire.”

name: double sided blunt staff
shortDescription: A balanced staff with weighted striking ends, useful for guards, monks, trainers, and travelers who want authority without a blade. The blunt design makes it easier to sell in stricter towns, though bruises rarely respect local law.
flavorText: “A democratic weapon: both ends are equally persuasive.”

name: greatsling
shortDescription: A large sling built for heavier stones and stronger arms, favored by hill fighters and siege-minded shepherds. It looks humble until used well, which is why veterans never laugh at one twice.
flavorText: “A pouch, two cords, and the ancient belief that distance improves courage.”

name: greatsling strong
shortDescription: A reinforced greatsling with sturdier cords and a tougher pouch, made to throw weight that would ruin a lesser sling. It sells best to practical fighters who enjoy cheap ammunition and expensive results.
flavorText: “Stone is free. Accuracy is not.”

name: throwing teeth
shortDescription: A packet of jagged throwing teeth, more unsettling than elegant and clearly meant for a buyer with unusual habits. Some are bone, some are carved substitutes, and all of them make appraisers reach for tongs.
flavorText: “Not every weapon needs to be beautiful. Some only need to be remembered.”

name: scarce holy scourge whip
shortDescription: A scarce scourge whip marked with holy symbols, bought by zealots, relic hunters, and collectors of uncomfortable devotional gear. Its market value rests in the uneasy gap between penance, ritual, and battlefield utility.
flavorText: “Faith has many instruments. The louder ones sell faster.”

name: market quarterstaff
shortDescription: A common market quarterstaff, straight, sturdy, and ready for guards, walkers, and apprentices with more confidence than training. It is inexpensive stock, but steady; every road produces new customers for a good stick.
flavorText: “A staff is just a stick until someone learns footwork.”

name: fine quarterstaff
shortDescription: A smooth, well-balanced quarterstaff with careful shaping and a pleasing hand feel. It appeals to disciplined fighters and teachers who know that a simple weapon made properly is never truly simple.
flavorText: “Fine wood, straight grain, and just enough polish to make practice feel official.”

name: strong quarterstaff
shortDescription: A heavy-duty quarterstaff reinforced for hard use, less graceful than a fine staff but far less likely to crack in a bad argument. Caravan guards and rural militias buy these by the bundle when trouble grows fashionable.
flavorText: “When subtlety fails, bring a better stick.”

name: scythe
shortDescription: A curved scythe normally meant for harvest, though markets have a way of teaching tools new reputations. Farmers inspect the haft; fighters inspect the angle; superstitious buyers inspect the room before touching it.
flavorText: “A field tool with a long shadow.”

name: fabled scythe of curses
shortDescription: A fabled scythe wrapped in stories of bad luck, ruined harvests, and owners who could never quite sell it fast enough. That reputation only raises the price among collectors, who are brave in direct proportion to their distance from the blade.
flavorText: “The curse may be nonsense. The surcharge is very real.”

name: fabled scythe of fire
shortDescription: A fabled scythe linked to burning fields and battle hymns sung too close to midnight. It is a dramatic piece, all curve and menace, and it attracts buyers who believe a weapon should announce itself like a bonfire.
flavorText: “Harvest with it, and even the weeds may beg for rain.”

name: scythe of ice
shortDescription: A cold-named scythe whose pale finish and crisp edge make it popular with northern warriors and theatrical collectors alike. It need not truly freeze anything to sell well; the right chill in the story is usually enough.
flavorText: “Some blades threaten heat. This one suggests a quiet winter.”

name: serpent scourge whip
shortDescription: A serpent-themed scourge whip with coiling decorations and a nasty silhouette, favored by duelists who prefer intimidation before contact. The craftsmanship ranges from devotional to theatrical, but either version looks dangerous in a locked case.
flavorText: “It hisses only when the seller is doing the demonstration properly.”

name: silver tomahawk
shortDescription: A tomahawk finished with bright silver fittings or plating, too fine for careless throwing and too useful to dismiss as decoration. It draws collectors, hunters, and nobles who insist their practical tools should still embarrass everyone else’s.
flavorText: “A small axe with a rich person’s confidence.”

name: sling
shortDescription: A simple sling of cord and pouch, cheap to buy and difficult to master. It remains reliable road stock because shepherds, scouts, and bored children all eventually discover that small stones become serious at speed.
flavorText: “The humblest weapon in the stall, until it leaves the hand.”

name: sling strong
shortDescription: A reinforced sling with tougher cords and a thicker pouch, meant for repeated use and rough travel. It is still plain, still affordable, and still capable of making armored men look upward with concern.
flavorText: “Small enough for a pocket, troublesome enough for a helmet.”

name: snakebite whip
shortDescription: A quick, narrow whip with a name that promises a sharp sting and a faster lesson. Performers like the drama, handlers like the reach, and tavern buyers like pretending they know how to use it.
flavorText: “Named for a snake, bought by peacocks.”

name: steel tomahawk
shortDescription: A practical steel tomahawk built for chopping, throwing, and the kind of camp work that becomes fighting without much notice. Its value is in honest balance and a head that survives both timber and trouble.
flavorText: “One tool for the woodpile, the trail, and the argument after dark.”

name: throwing net
shortDescription: A weighted throwing net used by hunters, guards, and beast handlers who prefer a target captured instead of carved. It takes skill to cast cleanly, but buyers appreciate gear that can end trouble without ruining the merchandise.
flavorText: “The best net turns motion into paperwork.”

name: flint
shortDescription: A hard piece of flint used for sparks, tool edges, and countless small acts of survival. It is modest stock, but no merchant mocks the item that starts fires when the sky has been raining insults all week.
flavorText: “Civilization begins again every time flint meets steel.”

name: fishing line
shortDescription: A coil of fishing line, light, easy to lose, and essential to anyone hoping water will become dinner. Good line sells quietly and often; bad line returns with complaints and stories about the fish that escaped.
flavorText: “Thin as a rumor, useful as a full plate.”

name: fishing pole
shortDescription: A simple fishing pole for riverbanks, docks, and travelers who believe patience can be converted into supper. It is not glamorous stock, but it moves steadily wherever roads cross water.
flavorText: “Some fortunes are pulled from the sea. This one starts with lunch.”

name: sickle
shortDescription: A curved sickle for cutting grain, herbs, and the occasional stubborn vine. Farmers judge the edge, herbalists judge the curve, and practical merchants judge how many villages nearby are preparing for harvest.
flavorText: “A small moon of steel for bringing fields down by the handful.”

name: trowel
shortDescription: A hand trowel used by gardeners, masons, diggers, and people with very specific secrets to bury. It is small, sturdy, and always in demand among those who work close to the ground.
flavorText: “Not every important tool looks important from standing height.”

name: pitchfork
shortDescription: A work-worn pitchfork suited to hay, muck, stable duty, and angry crowds that forgot to bring better weapons. It is honest farm stock with a surprising amount of civic history behind it.
flavorText: “Every village owns pitchforks. Every ruler hopes they stay in barns.”

name: handsaw
shortDescription: A handsaw with a toothed edge ready for planks, branches, and long afternoons of noisy labor. Carpenters inspect the set of the teeth; thieves inspect whether it can fit under a cloak.
flavorText: “Measure twice, cut once, complain loudly either way.”

name: plow
shortDescription: A sturdy plow for turning stubborn soil into something that might eventually forgive the farmer. It is heavy, plain, and valuable in the only way that matters: harvests begin behind it.
flavorText: “A kingdom can survive without jewels longer than it can survive without plows.”

name: hunting box trap
shortDescription: A box trap built to catch small animals alive, useful for hunters, cooks, and collectors of creatures best transported while annoyed but breathing. Its hinges and trigger matter more than its looks, though clean wood helps the price.
flavorText: “A polite trap, in that it closes the door before beginning negotiations.”

name: hunting foot trap
shortDescription: A foot trap for hunters who value certainty over elegance, with iron jaws and a mechanism that demands careful handling. It sells only with warnings, especially to buyers who smile too much while asking for a demonstration.
flavorText: “A trap is patient because it never has anywhere else to be.”

name: tomahawk
shortDescription: A compact tomahawk for chopping, throwing, and travel, light enough to carry and useful enough to justify the belt space. It is one of those tools that becomes a weapon without changing its expression.
flavorText: “Small axe, long list of excuses.”

name: scarce solid gold tomahawk
shortDescription: A scarce solid gold tomahawk whose usefulness is mostly ceremonial, unless the goal is to make every other weapon in the room feel underdressed. It is heavy, impractical, and wonderfully easy to price for collectors with loud coin purses.
flavorText: “A golden axe proves wealth can be sharpened, even if wisdom cannot.”

name: scythe of the necromancer
shortDescription: A grim scythe tied to necromancer tales, graveyard rumors, and buyers who lower their voices before making offers. Whether it holds true power or only dreadful theater, it should be wrapped carefully and never displayed beside cheerful farm tools.
flavorText: “Some harvests are best left uncounted.”

name: bomb
shortDescription: A sealed explosive charge sold with stern warnings, cautious paperwork, and a very steady hand. Miners, sappers, and reckless problem-solvers all ask for it, but wise merchants never let customers test enthusiasm indoors.
flavorText: “The fastest way to make a door, a hole, or a lifelong regret.”
